comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's almost midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of John Michell, first-rate sacred geometer and researcher into forgotten sciences, whose 1969 book The View Over Atlantis introduced leys and sacred geometry to the counterculture, and was a major influence on my insufficiently misspent youth. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar.

With that said, have at it! 

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. See you next week!***
 
[IMAGE:1]

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
What advice do you have for someone who's considering a move? What factors would determine where would be a good place to move, and what would determine a good time?

I plan to perform divination before making any move, but what other steps should I take?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If you know your way around astrology, do a relocation chart -- a birth chart for yourself, cast as though you'd been born at the place you plan to move to, at the same moment you were actually born (correcting for time zone differences if needed). See if the relocation does anything positive for your house placements -- for example, moving a benefic planet into one of the angular houses (1st, 4th, 7th, 10th) where it will be stronger, or moving a malefic out of one of the angular houses to weaken it. Then elect a time for the move in which Mercury and the Moon are in favorable aspect to the planet that rules your natal chart.

Lacking a background in astrology, use your preferred method of divination to do the equivalent.

replies.title: Wow! Thanks!
replies.content:
This is a keeper, and totally applicable to my case. The planet that rules my chart - would that be the one whose house is the sign that contains the cusp of the Tenth, i.e. Midheaven?

Kevin

replies.title: Re: Wow! Thanks!
replies.content: Nope. It's the planet that rules your ascendant.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
A few weeks ago I found a copy of Dan Brown's Inferno and gave it a read. To my horror the plot takes a turn where, instead of a genetically modified virus wiping out civilization, one third of the world becomes sterilized and the characters blandly say, "how lovely! We can keep on living our jet set lifestyles!" I find it one of the most morally bankrupt books I had ever read. Indeed, I caught whiffs of Engine Summer that still revolt me.

If Dan Brown serves as any sort of barometer of the psyche of the well-to-do, that would mean that many would be all too happy with a convenient plague to take hold so they could keep on living their extravagant lifestyle. My question -- with ebola now in the news again in a warzone with vaccinations rapidly drawing down -- is how much do you think these narratives play into the events unfolding. I remember with the ebola outbreaks a few years ago there was a very odd clown feeling to it -- with the power spraying of ebola vomit onto pedestrians in Texas. Perhaps people are just often stupid, but I also wonder if the apparent feverish desire for plague, not to mention ebola-chan and the like, contribute to these uncanny events. I'm curious your thoughts on this JMG; do you think that there likely is an occult dimension to the unfolding of events, and if do you see useful steps that people reading this blog could perhaps mitigate this insanity?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The sort of thinking you saw in Inferno is very widespread among the privileged, though it's rare to find anyone actually admitting out loud that they want to commit genocide against the poor. It's simply that (for example) the climate change activists who insist the government ought to force people to stop using fossil fuels show a complete lack of interest in decreasing their own use of fossil fuels, and well-off people who get bent out of shape about overpopulation routinely have children, i.e., are part of the problem rather than part of the solution.

The difficulty they face is that the rest of the population is aware of this. That's an important part of what's driving the current mess in France: the poor and working classes aren't willing to see the cost of environmental reforms piled on their backs, while their soi-disant betters still get to have the extravagant lifestyles they think they deserve.

Are there occult dimensions to all this? Yes, but I don't think it's a matter of deliberate projection of narratives on the part of the privileged. Listening to the frankly weird detachment from reality one hears so often from the well-to-do these days, I think they're caught up in something much bigger than they are, and quite possibly much bigger than they can imagine. Giambattista Vico, writing about "the course the nations run," notes that the historical cycle begins with necessity and ends in madness; we're seeing that latter phase taking shape around us right now.

replies.title: Bad faith in action...
replies.content:
I’ve noticed that the lowest tier of the would-be intelligentsia and the previously discussed bottom rung of the managerial class often almost admit their blood lust; although, they tend to put their euphemisms in hardened Orwellium alloy boxes such as, “If we could just get (insert hated group) out of the way…”. Of course, pointing out that “out of the way” means trashing the rule of law and unpersoning the offending demographic produces emotional eruptions fit for the golden age of animation, including calling me crazy for even considering thinking of such a horrible misinterpretation of a clearly rational position.
If there is an occult aspect, what’s a good Green Wizard to do besides avoid such people, bite his tongue and drown his sorrows in off-brand soda pop?

replies.title: Re: Bad faith in action...
replies.content: Avoid such people and banish regularly. You might also consider wearing a protective amulet when you're going to be around them.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: There's not much that can be done for them, but how do we keep from being dragged into their madness?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Banish regularly and pursue an active spiritual life. Oh, and throw out your television if you haven't done so already.

comments.title: Witch Bottles
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
I tried to find a witch-bottle spell at the Lucky Mojo Company but my computer skills are not up to the task.

I looked at a number of youtube videos on the subject and came up with a number of commonalities: one's own urine in the bottle along with 9 pins, shards of glass or mirror shards, lots of salt, red string, and protective herbs.

One person suggested burning a black candle on top of the bottle with the name of the person to be protected against.

I only wish to be protected from malign magic. Would you recommend modifying this recipe to achieve the desired result
Maxine

replies.title: Re: Witch Bottles
replies.content: The standard version I know involves a collection of sharp iron or steel objects -- pins, needles, nails, broken chunks of metal, it's all good -- and then fill with your own urine. Salt would be a useful admixture, and of course binding it with a red thread is very classic. The goal here is to have something that has your energy signature on it to lure hostile magic toward an easy target, and discharge uselessly on the iron. I wouldn't worry about the black candle -- just make sure it's well hidden, and not within twenty feet of anyplace you spend much time (i.e., not under your bed or something).

replies.title: Re: Witch Bottles
replies.content:
Dear JMG,
Many thanks for the very clear and informative explanation. I feel sure I can do a good job now and if you ever put out a book on HooDoo Magic, I will pre-order a copy.

I gave my sister, a lapsed Wican, a copy of your Druid Magic Handbook and she is delighted with it and wants to buy a copy for everyone she knows.

Still on the subject of books, I have been spending a lot of time in my Inner Grove during meditation as I think better there. I send my image? to the Grove and can feel it leaving me and returning. Is this the Astral body? If it is, could you recommend a good book for beginners of the safe way to interact with this type of magic?
Yours with much gratitude,
Maxine

replies.title: Re: Witch Bottles
replies.content: That's astral vision rather than astral projection; it's easy and quite safe, especially if you do it in an open grove. I don't know of a book specifically about it, but you don't really need one -- practice it regularly and you'll get good at it.

comments.title: New Thought, Emmet Fox and the One God
comments.content:
I am contemplating taking up the ideas of some reputable New Thought author and applying them with a view to improving my life. I began the programme of the Order of Essenes that you launched some time ago, but fell off that horse several weeks ago. To speak truth, I’m not sure that it is the one for me. What is your opinion of - or, to your knowledge, what is the reputation of - the writings of Emmet Fox, in particular his “The Golden Key”?

In a related question, I’ve noticed that Fox and other New Thought authors make frequent reference to the Bible and - in Fox’s case at any rate - God. As a polytheist, how do parse this sort of thing when dealing with such works? Clearly I’m going to have to work that one out.

You’ve mentioned in the past that when applying for membership in the brotherhood of Masons you had to affirm belief in a supreme deity. Would you mind saying here what that deity might be? It’s possible I may consider joining in the future, and am wondering how one with pagan, Hermetic and Hellenic tendencies might manage.

Kevin

replies.title: Re: New Thought, Emmet Fox and the One God
replies.content:
1) I can't help you there. I've read only a very few New Thought authors -- Burks Hamner (the author of the Order of Essenes course) and William Walker Atkinson -- and don't have enough of a background in the broader tradition to offer advice.

2) The Masons don't specify which supreme being you have to worship, just that you have to worship one. Since most polytheist faiths do have a deity who is supreme -- consider the famous passage from The Iliad where Zeus talks about the golden chain -- this isn't a particular problem for most polytheists. The one thing is that the label "God" is pretty consistently used in Masonic literature and ritual for whatever supreme being you worship, and you pretty much have to be okay with that noun.

One suggestion: try to find a lodge with some members who are occultists. They can brief you on what the specific local requirements are and what the other brethren can handle hearing about.

replies.title: Re: New Thought, Emmet Fox and the One God
replies.content:
"the famous passage from The Iliad where Zeus talks about the golden chain"

If JMG allows me to quote The War Nerd Iliad:

"ZEUS IS SICK OF THIS WAR. Nine years of killing, no results. He calls the
family to conference and lays down the law: “No more meddling with the
Greeks or Trojans, trying to tilt the battle. No more interference from any of you gods … or goddesses.”
As he says “or goddesses,” he looks hard at Hera and Athena. Athena is
sulking, refusing to look him in the eye. Zeus says, “Maybe you all need to
be reminded that I could throw the whole bunch of you into Tartarus, one
layer below Hell. Would you like that? I threw the Titans down there, and all of you put together aren’t as strong as they were.”
He hunches over the table to let them see his strength. “Suppose we were
to play tug-o-war, with all of you dragging me down toward earth. I’d pull
you all up into the sky instead, and the whole Earth and ocean with you. I’m the papa here, and don’t you forget it.”
Athena speaks up: “Papa, we all know you have final say. But you can’t blame Mama and me for being sad when we see so many brave Greek warriors dying down there at Troy.”
Zeus is about to rage at her when she smiles, suddenly girlish, and pipes
up, “Papa, what if we didn’t interfere directly, but just made some suggestions to the Greeks, a little advice?”
Zeus can’t help laughing. “Just suggestions, huh? Ah, girl, don’t worry, I’d never hurt you.”
He turns to the rest of them: “But as for the rest of you: No more meddling in the war, or you’ll regret it.”
Zeus descends to the hill above Troy, leaving his horses and chariot in the
clouds to wait for him. He sits in his temple to watch the day’s fight. He can see the walls of Troy and the far campfires of the Greeks. If he wants, he can see every pebble in the darkest cellar of Troy, each goat-hair strand of the Greek tents. If he felt like it, he could snake his way through the wet interior of every Greek and Trojan skull, and if he took a dislike to any of the ideas sparking in that wet brain-mush, he could turn the owner of that thought into a carbonized husk with one lightning bolt."

replies.title: Re: New Thought, Emmet Fox and the One God
replies.content: That's the one! Now tell me that Zeus wasn't the Supreme Being in classic Greek religion...

replies.title: Re: New Thought, Emmet Fox and the One God
replies.content:
It might be used to argue that actually smart good-looking goddesses rule - Zeus does whatever he wants, but who decides what Zeus wants?

Also from TWNI:

"NOW ZEUS HAS TO KILL even more of the Greeks. His first thought, a
painful, wincing one: “Hera’s not going to like this.” His wife and sister
Hera always knows what he’s up to, and she’s soft on the Greeks.
She’s permanently mad at him anyway, because he’s just an old horndog
pretending to be in command when he can’t even command his own penis.
These people were very down on lust. That’s one of the ways they weren’t like us. We love lust. They didn’t. It was too dangerous, and it gave women too much power. So lust is a bad thing in this story. To these people, a real man doesn’t get led around by his [penis]. And if he does, he’s not a man at all. A stud, to their way of thinking, is a sissy. And above all, a sissy/stud is dangerous, capable of wiping out an entire city.
The man who started this whole war was a stud, a Trojan prince named Paris, fitting for a man with the sexual ego of Pepé Le Pew. The only reason he didn’t drive a Porsche or wear Ray-Bans was because the infrastructure wasn’t there yet. He’d have defected to Malibu in a second if the airport had been ready. And this princeling, Paris, had the chance to judge a beauty contest of three female gods. And that’s what got Troy besieged.
It was all a plot by the goddess Hate. She is a great and ancient goddess around the shores of the Mediterranean, then and now. Read the news if you
doubt it."

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
(Tolkienguy)

How much overlap is there between Paths of Wisdom and Learning Ritual Magic? I am enjoying the first and was thinking of buying the second, but one of the Amazon reviews of LRM said that it only has the LBRP and Middle Pillar rituals, which are in PoW.

On a related note, what are people's opinions on Modern Magick by Donald Kraig? He seems to cover a wide variety of topics, but the excerpts of his book on Amazon had a really chatty writing style I found off-putting, and he comes off as less Christianity-friendly than JMG is (in particular, he has a whole chapter on "Tantra and Sex Magick", which sets off a whole choir of alarm bells in my head).

And last question-in the section in PoW on Tiphareth, you mention that some Christian Cabalists have equated Tiphareth to Christ, then dismiss this. Who would these people be, and are any of their books still in print? Does Gareth Knight make this argument in hus books on Cabala? I'm asking because the way you describe Tiphareth in that section, as a sort of mediator between the more human lower levels of the tree and the divine upper levels, lines up almost exactly with Christ's role in Christianity (the God-man through whom we can relate to the otherwise unknowable Father). On a somewhat related note, toward the beginning of PoW, you reproduce two images depicting Eden and the Fall from a Cabalistic point of view. Has any Christian Cabalist done a third diagram, depicting the reversal of the Fall with Christ's crucifixion?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Learning Ritual Magic is a guide for beginners, and it takes things very much a step at a time. Those are the only two rituals it includes, but it also has a lot of other exercises, practices, and themes for meditation.

2) To my mind, Kraig's book is a very mixed bag, and I don't tend to recommend it.

3) Please remember that Paths of Wisdom was the first book I ever published; it first appeared 22 years ago, and most of the writing was done some years before that. I had a very limited grasp of Christian Cabala in those days, and made some mistakes. You can find the equation of Christ to Tiphareth in almost any work of Christian Cabala, going back to Pico della Mirandola in the fifteenth century; yes, Knight among others discusses that. As for the third diagram, you'll find it in The Golden Dawn by Israel Regardie, in the illustrations -- it's the diagram on the lid of the Pastos.

comments.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Wear a sapphire. Its energies will very nicely counterbalance any difficulties you might get from a diamond.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Would an artificial sapphire work as well as a natural one?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I don't know. These rules were developed long before artificial sapphires were a thing. You could certainly experiment!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear Mr Greer,

I was wondering, in your understanding and experience, is there something that should be the most important focus in a person's life?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That depends totally on the individual. Each person comes into each life with a unique combination of fate (the legacy of previous lives) and destiny (the goals the higher self seeks to express). Thus what should be the most important focus in your life is almost certainly different from what should be the most important focus in mine -- and, yes, figuring out what you want to focus on is an important part of being alive.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: How useful is the South/North Node in understanding past and current life path? Is this analogous, or an indicator, to Cos.Doc discription of the 12 rays/zodiacal movement across the planes, particularly of interest to those electing to join the Path? Thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I haven't started to work with the Nodes yet. I'm still learning astrology -- I only got into declinations a year or so ago!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
In years of casual use, I've only used transits and progressions of natal charts--no deep study yet I'm embarrassed to say. As an aside, it seems your ingress reports are at ~100% with the recent announcement of major prison reform legislation.

I looked into the Nodes because the North Node interpretation was listed first on an app used to plot a chart. The reading was a profound "aha" moment--maybe just because it was novel, but a gift nonetheless. So you (or your readers) might be in for a treat when you get to them :)

They're easy to look up and assuming it's not some new fangled untested thing, they may provide useful insight into the central task of an incarnation, for those seeking such.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Fair enough. They're on the get-to list, I just haven't gotten to them yet.

comments.title: Daily prayer
comments.content: Do you have a daily prayer practice? If so, what form does it take? Do you say individual prayers to each of the deities to which you're devoted, or just certain ones depending on the day and circumstances? When does your prayer time happen in relation to your daily magical practices? Do you do it right after your daily ritual and meditation, or some other time?

replies.title: Re: Daily prayer
replies.content: My daily prayer practice is integrated into my ritual and meditative work, and focuses on two specific deities. I'm not particularly gifted at prayer -- that's one of the reasons I'm a mage rather than a mystic -- and the form it takes is a period of silent freeform prayer ending with a traditional Druid prayer spoken aloud.

replies.title: Re: Daily prayer
replies.content: What would it mean to be gifted at prayer?

replies.title: Re: Daily prayer
replies.content: Like being gifted at anything else -- it's quicker to learn and you get good results easily.

replies.title: Re: Daily prayer
replies.content: Okay that's uncanny; my prayer devotion is exactly the same. There are two Welsh druid deities on whom I focus, and I end with the Druids' Prayer. Do you pray while still in your meditative state, or do you end your meditation and "come back" before beginning prayer?

replies.title: Re: Daily prayer
replies.content:
Great minds think alike, I guess. ;-)

I finish my meditation, then pray, but you can do it either way.

comments.title: What novel?
comments.content:
Good day JMG,

This is not about occultism but I'll ask it anyway.
I read somewhere that there is a novel you prefer when compared to Tolkien's The Lord of the Rings.
What would that be?
Thanks

replies.title: Re: What novel?
replies.content:
The Well at the World's End by William Morris. It's not without its flaws, but to my taste it's a far more mature and thoughtful novel than Tolkien's, and its high moments -- and it has quite a few of them -- are of stunning power. It's all the more astonishing in that Morris quite literally invented fantasy fiction, and The Well at the World's End is the second fantasy novel ever written.

I also highly recommend his novel The Water of the Wondrous Isles. It was published in 1897, and the woman who's its central character is far more active, realistic and interesting than most of the heroines of our allegedly more liberated day.

replies.title: Re: What novel?
replies.content: The second fantasy novel ever written? I'm curious, which was the first?

replies.title: Re: What novel?
replies.content: The Wood Beyond the World, also by Morris. It's very much a first attempt, though quite readable.

replies.title: Re: What novel?
replies.content: For people other than JMG, who surely knows: "Tolkien considered much of his literary work to have been inspired by an early reading of Morris, even suggesting that he was unable to better Morris's work; the names of characters such as "Gandolf" and the horse Silverfax appear in The Well at the World's End.".

replies.title: Re: What novel?
replies.content: Ah, but in Morris' novel Gandolf is a villain, and note also the polarity between Shadowfax and Silverfax. I've long suspected that The Lord of the Rings is basically Tolkien's argument with Morris: an argument at once political (Tolkien was a reactionary, Morris a socialist), religious (Tolkien was a conservative Catholic, Morris ran with occultists and had no time for Christian orthodoxy), and moral (Tolkien's moral notions were steadfastly Victorian, Morris' anything but).

replies.title: Re: What novel?
replies.content:
... thanks, Grandpa!

- S. T. Silva.

comments.title: Organic Items in Amulets
comments.content:
When organic items are used to make an amulet, does decay makes the amulet stop working over time? If so, are there ways to process these items that extend useful life of the amulet?

Life us an apt word to use; maybe my thought is too Faustian. Machines are quite like amulets, minus life. First you use Fire to remove Water, killing the parts to avoid decay; then you combine the parts to build a machine. A form of necromancy with an elemental imbalance. Another straw in the pile of "this will not end well."

replies.title: Re: Organic Items in Amulets
replies.content:
1) Yes, very much so. That's why it's standard to use dried herbs rather than fresh ones, and otherwise to make use of materials that will last.

2) Well put.

replies.title: Re: Organic Items in Amulets
replies.content:
What's the difference between what's done to the herbs and what's done to the machine parts?

- S. T. Silva.

replies.title: Re: Organic Items in Amulets
replies.content: I encourage you to meditate on that.

replies.title: Re: Organic Items in Amulets
replies.content: Noted. Thanks.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I bought Mystery Teachings from the Living Earth from the Book Depository, since the linked publisher only shipped to US. Do you get money from that?

And in starting the meditations, I realized quickly that I know very little about where my things come from. How much effort should I put into finding out? Is it enough to know I don't know, or should I read what I find on the internet, or send e-mails to try and get more information?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes. They're a legit bookseller, and buy their stock from publishers.

2) I'd encourage you to do some reading and asking around, and learn at least a little about where things come from.

comments.title: Retrotopia/Rust Belt
comments.content:
JMG:

1) I've read enough of Retrotopia to wonder: did you deliberately craft it to be a reader's tour of a sanely harmonized human organism? Carr's destinations in the book to me are explicit references to some of the parts of the human being. I gave no spoilers! Perhaps I am merely 'appropriating' your work for my own allegorical purposes? Bwah ha ha ha. In that case I'm being rarely fashionable. :)

2) In previous posts or comments you wrote that you bet on the Rust Belt in our general collapse and it did not go so well in some ways. Perhaps I'm misremembering but I don't think so. If I'm remembering correctly, and if you do not mind, then would you list a couple of general reasons why that didn't work out for you? I live in rural Michigan, and, in some ways, my part of it is a field of blocked opportunities and willful and unwillful ignorance to such degrees that I'm a borderline hermit when it comes to civic life and I'd rather not be. I'm wondering if some of our experiences of trends in once stable but now decaying industrial zones are common experiences.

Stay Salty,

Saturn's Pet

replies.title: Re: Retrotopia/Rust Belt
replies.content:
1) Hmmm! No, I didn't intend that, but it doesn't surprise me that it worked out this way.

2) Yes, it was a gamble, and no, it didn't work the way I'd hoped it would. Partly that was an effect of local problems with pollution that put some serious strain on my and my wife's health; partly the opiate epidemic hit hard and caused some very problematic shifts in the community; and partly Amtrak was looking at closing down the train station where I was, which would have left me with no way to get anywhere else. On the bright side, we seem to have dodged the civil war I thought we were going to be facing, and so hiding in the hills was a little less necessary than I'd expected.

replies.title: Re: Retrotopia/Rust Belt
replies.content: What kind of pollution, if I may ask?

replies.title: Re: Retrotopia/Rust Belt
replies.content: There was an accident no one was willing to talk about at a factory upwind a few dozen miles that nobody was willing to talk about either. It was after that happened that the health problems started. I don't know what was involved, and I doubt I ever will.

replies.title: Re: Retrotopia/Rust Belt
replies.content: I live on the other side of the lake from you and I get the same feeling here. It's like the whole region is in a great struggle to find it's direction. From what I'm able to determine there are two major factions active politically - one that wants to turn the lakes into the next lake front property for the coastal elites and the other that wants to turn us into a resource mine for the coastal elites. But underneath that is this great potential force that's slowly waking up. What will happen once it wakes is anyone's game.

replies.title: Re: Retrotopia/Rust Belt
replies.content:
syfen:

If you're responding to my comment, then hello, and thanks for the inflow of news and analysis. I don't know exactly what state or province you're in, but I'm guessing Ontario because we in my part of Michigan refer to Ontario as the other side of the lake. :) Perhaps not though.

Have you read Retrotopia? Its Lakeland Republic is the freshest and sanest vision of what might be in our region that I have encountered in fiction or non-fiction.

Do you have any thoughts on the upcoming activation of the UN Global Compact for Migration? If that bundle of legal joy matures as its makers intend, then I also expect many parts and peoples of our region to experience the thrills and chills caused by rapid demographic shifts while being mined and groomed for resettlement.

Good thing we're used to wild weather!

Saturn's Pet

comments.title: etheric solar charging
comments.content: A couple of questions regarding same...how early can the charging be done? Sunrise, as soon as the sun is over the horizon... also, do you think that seasonal affective disorder might be caused by a depleted etheric body and, if so, the charging might help? I'm planning on trying it so if I have any notable results I'll post them come springtime. Hmmm, guess that's three questions... etheric solar charging is mentioned in our host's book 'Paths of Wisdom', in case anyone was wondering. Thanks much!

replies.title: Re: etheric solar charging
replies.content:
1) As soon as the sun is visible over the horizon.

2 & 3) I don't know. It might be worth giving it a try, though.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
(Also fairly cute! Okay, I'll stop being me now. :P)

Speculative/fiction-writing question: given how the Tree of Life and Adam Kadmon intersect, would you think it likely that non-human mages (whether magic animals or different forms of life altogether) would have their own versions? Or would they have a completely different magical practice?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'm sure they'd have a very different scheme. For example, we think of "up" as good as a legacy of millions of years of primate life where climbing a tree was a source of safety. An intelligent animal that didn't have that evolutionary experience -- say, a descendant of llamas -- probably wouldn't think of the spiritual as "higher" than the material. Come up with something that fits your species of choice, and have fun with it!

comments.title: Kirlian Photography
comments.content:
Hi,

Would you know if there are any links between kirlian photography and etheric or astral bodies?

Thanks

Karim

replies.title: Re: Kirlian Photography
replies.content: Good question. It would take a lot of systematic research by people with a good working knowledge of the etheric plane to settle that.

comments.title: Mushroms astrological correspondence
comments.content:
Any idea about the astrological associations of mushroms? Im not even talking about the alucinogenous like amanita muscaria but of the edible ones, like pleurotus ostreatus.

comments.title: Sewing Needles and Acedia: a Data Point
comments.content:
I had been trying to scale back my stupid-internet-timewasting for a while, and gave your sharp-objects idea a whirl. I took a sewing needle, and just set it on the hinge on the back of the computer monitor. I thought eh, if nothing else, I'll know it's there, and it'll remind me I'm trying to leash this beast.

A couple of weeks in, the LAN port broke. We ordered a new one, replaced it... and I removed the sewing needle. Goal accomplished: I was without internet for a week, except jumping on husband's device to furtively check email. Not sure I can afford to replace computer parts every few weeks, though!

Looking now for a small icon of St. Evagrius to replace it (he was the first of the Desert Fathers to address the topic of acedia). Perhaps he will be more merciful and not break my computer.

John--

Re your response to the question re Inferno and the idea of the privileged being caught up in something larger than they realize or understand.

To the extent that we are talking about occult forces here and the interactions of will, fate, and destiny, but on a scale of societies rather than individuals, are those mitigating actions possible? replies.title: (no subject)

comments.title: Heathen Golden Dawn
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I completed "Learning Ritual Magic" several months ago, and since then have been unsure where I want to take my magical practice.

I've had some interest in the Celtic GD, and also in continuing with your other books like "Circles of Power."

However, I also came across some posts about your Heathen GD project where you mention following a path that really "clicks" with you, with gods you are comfortable with. I've had mixed feelings about the Judeo-Christian symbolism. Sometimes I find interest in the parts that don't remind me as much of "stock-Christianity," but I also have some resistance towards it due to some negative associations from my conservative upbringing.

Although I've not studied much heathen material, I've felt a pull towards it and am wondering if that would be worth exploring further.

How would you recommend I do this? Maybe start with the heathen LBRP you posted, and see if it resonates with me?

I'm a little cautious about jumping headfirst into a new system after spending a while with the Judeo-Christian GD and not being sure its the best path for me (I took longer much longer than 9 months with LRM). So I'm thinking just exploring a bit may be best for now and seeing what works best.

If I find the heathen material resonates, where would you suggest I go from there?

Thanks,
Ross

replies.title: Re: Heathen Golden Dawn
replies.content: I'd definitely suggest exploring a bit. If the heathen material resonates, you might still be better off going on to Circles of Power for now, because Sven and I still have a lot of work to do before the Heathen Golden Dawn project is ready for publication.

replies.title: Re: Heathen Golden Dawn
replies.content:

Speaking of which, did my last update make it past the svartalfar? I am curious of your thoughts regarding the last ritual. Also, perhaps these heathens deserve a 1st degree opening/closing for their Yule present...?

replies.title: Re: Heathen Golden Dawn

I need to finish a bit of research and then the four elemental pentagram rituals and the elemental workings should be easy to draft. Ross

replies.title: Re: Heathen Golden Dawn
replies.content: Circles of Power isn't a study course like Learning Ritual Magic, it's more of a textbook. As for the symbolism, one of the things you'll get to learn as you proceed is that magic doesn't care whether you like a given set of symbolism -- if you do the work you get the benefits.

comments.title: Can relatives still influence after they die?
comments.content: My mother, her sisters and various other relatives were always pathologically resentful of others’ accumulating successes.  Especially, their siblings or children.  One was supposed to be successful enough not to be a burden, but if one went much beyond that, the passive aggressive (or outright aggressive) behavior was meant to pound you back into their approved place for you.  So as a naturally talented and serious practicing artist, I found that once I got beyond my “station,” the snark got really nasty from those who should have been most supportive.  They are dead now, but I find that within a few hours of good news or a success, I will be gripped with some kind of pain in my lower limbs.  It feels like someone is trying to “cut me off at the knees” to prevent me from moving forward.  Until I started reading Magic Mondays, I always thought it was psychological on my part.  Now I’m not so sure.  My question is – can individuals keep behaving badly and having this type of influence after they die?  If so, what should I do?  I usually just tell them to “F**k Off, it is now my time, so get over it.”  That actually helps for a while.

replies.title: Re: Can relatives still influence after they die?
replies.content: It depends very much on circumstances. Most likely, the experience you're having is psychological in nature, but if one or more of your dead relatives got stuck in certain intermediate stages in the after-death process, they might be able to harass you. Regular practice of banishing rituals such as the Sphere of Protection or the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram will help; telling them to buzz off, as you're doing, will also help. More complex ways of dealing with this sort of thing require serious training in ceremonial magic.

comments.title: Practice with young children in the house
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I've seen your stern warnings in these Magic Monday discussions about practicing with young children in the same house. The limit I've seen is 5-7 years old.

I have a 4 month old. I want to take your warnings to heart, but I would still like to progress on the Path.

In one of your responses, you mentioned not to follow Learning Ritual Magic with young children in the house.

Is the CGD a safe path to start? Any particular practices I should avoid?

Overall, am I safe with the three foundations of occult training - divination, basic banishing, and discursive mediation?

Any potential issues I should look out for? Any safeguards I should employ? Any signs that something could be amiss?

Much thanks for this forum and your time.

replies.title: Re: Practice with young children in the house
replies.content:
No, The Celtic Golden Dawn should not be practiced with young children in the house. When you became a parent, you took on a responsibility that means your own wants and wishes have to go on the back burner for a while. Raising your child is the path you've taken up; follow it without haggling, and when your child's five or six years old you can look at taking up magic.

For now, I suggest meditation, divination, and study, in the very limited free time you'll have with a young child in the house.

replies.title: Re: Practice with young children in the house
replies.content:
Understood. Unfortunate to delay my practice, but I'm wholeheartedly committed to my current path.

I will focus on meditation, divination, and study.

One quick follow-up - what falls into the "study" category?

Read, just not practice?

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Practice with young children in the house
replies.content: Yep. Read to your heart's content. This would be a good time to get a solid familiarity with magical literature and with whatever cycles of mythology you favor -- not least because reading myths and legends to children is the best way, bar none, to make sure their imaginations have good rich fare to feed on.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What is the Druid stance on Pornography? Does it have any kind of magical properties or effects?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's a form of entertainment. As with any other form of entertainment, some people get obsessive about it and can take that obsession to destructive extremes, but I don't know of any magical reason why looking at pictures or videos of people making love should be any more problematic than looking at pictures or videos of people playing football or debating Brexit.

The notion that sex is somehow more morally loaded than any other human act, and can only be approached from behind lead shielding and heavy theological padding, is to my mind one of the more unhelpful hangovers from Christianity. It's an ordinary human activity, folks, and most other cultures treat it as such.

Should I avoid selecting themes from these books for meditation, or is it okay to meditate on them?

replies.content: Druidry is not so fragile that you have to swathe your mind in plastic wrap to keep from coming into contact with other ideas! Of course you can meditate on themes from such books as part of your Druid training; I went through Levi's Doctrine and Ritual of High Magic as part of mine.

comments.title: Puritans
comments.content: Today I discovered that the Puritans had the dogma of "2 births". The first was obviously ones biological birth. The 2nd birth was when one became of age one was sent to wonder around until they witnessed 3 miracles. Then you could return home and witness in front of the church the 3 miracles you saw, and become a full member of the community.  Very much like a "vision quest".

replies.title: Re: Puritans
replies.content: Yep. The land here in North America has its natural spiritual forms, and people who live here will tend to be drawn into those forms.

replies.title: Re: Puritans
replies.content: I’m becoming curious about Christian druidry but do not wish to leave the Church, and couldn’t if I wanted to seeing as how it already left me years ago.  What do you think?  If I investigated druidry would I be entangling myself in a Gordian knot of conflicting religious beliefs?

replies.title: Re: Puritans
replies.content: Not at all. Christian Druidry is a well-established tradition going back to the beginning of the Druid Revival; that's why I included Christian saints and angels in The Druidry Handbook, for example. The Christian Druids I've met tend to pay close attention to the first chapter of the Gospel of John, which identifies Christ as the aspect of God that created the world of nature, and go from there.

replies.title: Re: Puritans
replies.content:
"Two births" was not only a Puritan doctrine, but a Calvinist one generally, which was elaborated during the 1500s and 1600s in reaction against an older Roman Catholic (and Lutheran) doctrine or sacramental rebirth through baptism. Usually it is referred to as "Regeneration"; there's a wikipedia article on it under that word.

However, it did not usually involve any sort of wandering from home until the person had "seen the light." Occasionally a male--very very rarely, a female--might do this, but it wasn't the norm.

I suspect that your source may have confused by the Pennsylvania German custom (among the Old Order Amish) of "Rumspringen," whereby a young man is given the use of a horse and buggy to go out and freely explore the secular world on his own. (The theory is that if he does this and then comes back, then his commitment to his Church's discipline will be all the stronger.) The Amish are not Calvinists at all, but have European Anabaptist roots.

Sometimes Johnny Appleseed is mentioned in this connection, but he was a committed Swedenborgian, not a Puritan at all.

comments.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
This may have come up in the past, but after some recent events I'm curious what your perspective is on "synchronistic" or connected events, coincidences, etc. If you've written about it before, please just point me in that direction!

Since beginning to read your blogs, a strange kind of journey has taken place here -- after some deliberation, I decided to order The Celtic Golden Dawn, but thought I'd check in on my Welsh heritage first, due to the Welsh deities involved (I know it's not necessary, I was just curious). It ends up I have a line going back to the Wynne family of North Wales (my mother's maiden name is Winn) and Gwydir Castle there. As soon as I mentioned it, my mother was intrigued; her father had only ever said they were "English" in heritage. She was immediately searching online everything she could find about Gwydir Castle, and I got her a book by the current owners for her birthday, which she read ravenously. She's said just knowing about the place has given her a great sense of peace, like filled in a blank in her life, and even though she's never really wanted to travel overseas before (I've always felt deeply connected to the British Isles and have been there twice) she would very much like to visit Gwydir someday.

During all this, Welsh music, food, traditions, etc. have seemed to pop up all around us (not to mention me stumbling through Welsh words with my newborn practice in the mornings.)

I've always thought the "Law of Attraction" a bit overblown, or even "we draw to us what we are" etc. I've had a lot of this kind of experience throughout my life, though, things just popping up and being remarkably important to me, and part of my reason for continuing to explore paths like the Celtic Golden Dawn is because of these weird coincidences. Maybe we never know why or where they come from, they just keep leading us forward?

By the way, the house and I keep talking, and I've been expressing a lot of gratitude for the warm water, and the cozy rooms, and so forth, and it has seemed to be a lot happier lately.

Diane

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'd encourage you to read Jung's essay "Synchronicity" sometime. He argued that it's one of the major processes of the cosmos, as important as cause and effect. I tend to think he's quite correct in that.

comments.title: Diamonds and SOP
comments.content:
Don't know if you get back to the previous post on diamonds in engagement and wedding rings, so I'm asking my question here again and expand it a little bit:

The question, that comes to my mind is: How powerful is this effect? There's a lot of space between "major disruptions to any relationship" and "can make a good relationship slightly worse, might break one that was on the verge of breaking anyway"... Steering a bit off-topic: Is it possible to reach a state where one is not susceptible to such effects anymore? A text about feng-shui comes to my mind... basically it said, if I recall it correctly, that if you were "developed" and aware enough (I suppose "developed" was meant in a spiritual way here) you would not need to use feng shui anymore. I heard about that quite a while ago and can't give you any link, unfortunately. Anyhow, the question boils down to:

How powerful can the effect of easily is the effect of the diamond ring be and do different people have a different susceptibility to natural magic or magic in general? Can (should?) a state be reached in which you do not have to have a compulsory reaction to its effects?

Second question, if I may: What about the negation in the SOP "WITHOUT harm for ..." Should one not avoid negations? If so, why is it not a problem here (is it not a problem here?)?

Thanks and greetings,
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Diamonds and SOP
replies.content:
1) How powerful the effect is depends on a galaxy of variables, including the specific energy of the stone and the specific energy of the person wearing it. Of course you can get to a state where such effects don't influence you -- that's one of the goals of magical training, the state where you perceive consciously the various magical influences that are impinging on you, and choose deliberately which of them you want to accept and which of them you want to let go tumbling past into the void.

2) If I were writing the SoP from scratch I wouldn't have phrased it that way. Sometimes it's more advantageous to use the existing wording, because it allows you to tap into the egregor.

comments.title: breaking a love charm cast by someone else towards me?
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Can you suggest any remedies for breaking, dispelling, banishing or otherwise “getting rid of” a love charm someone is directing at me? I became aware of it earlier this year through working with a healer - and that explained SO much!!! - and this poor person seems rather obsessed with me, though I have moved on. I admit I would like to clear his obsession, though I realize that may not be within my power. I dislike that I'm so irritated at this point that I think of him as an idiot, because really...he's just another wounded human being.

Ill effects for me (despite months of lots and lots and lots of daily banishing, even focused on that relationship) include repetitive/obsessive thinking about him and the drama triangle he drew me into for a time.

I am proactive about daily attending to whatever part of me might be still reaching out to him, and I redirect my thoughts whenever I notice I'm getting drawn in, but... *sigh* Is there anything more I can do to at least break the love charm part of this?

thank you for any guidance, and may all beings benefit

replies.title: Re: breaking a love charm cast by someone else towards me?
replies.content:
I can think of two different things that might be helpful for you to do. You only need to do one and will probably have to modify it to fit your current practice.

If part of your banishing meditation includes erecting or maintaining a shield at the outer boundary of your aura, you could visualize the outer facing surface of the shield as a mirror that bounces energy from the outside back to the sender. This is a short term measure, because you don't want to live inside a sealed environment that no energy can get through, but if what is being sent your way is an influence you dislike, bounce it back to the sender.

The other idea is like the witch's bottle discussed above, but on an energy level. The Berkeley Psychic Institute teaches, or used to teach, an energy meditation to prepare for doing clairvoyant readings. The middle of the meditation includes visualizing a beautiful rose in front of oneself, collecting any energy in one's aura that came from other people and dumping it into the rose, "blowing the rose up", and sending the energy back to wherever it came from. When I was being taught this meditation, I was reluctant to blow up the beautiful rose I had imagined. But after blowing it up and returning the energies to senders, you put up another rose in front of you. I was told that people who are sending unasked-for energy your way will mistake the rose for you and it will catch the energy. You blow that rose up and replace it, daily or oftener as needed. The rose can be any color or type.

The rose visualization is more complicated than the shield-mirror, and for a clairvoyantly ungifted person like myself it took a lot of practice, but it has the advantage that setting up a decoy doesn't get in the way of beneficial energies from outside that you want to receive. It just catches the controlling ones.

replies.title: Re: breaking a love charm cast by someone else towards me?
replies.content: Thank you, Deborah!

replies.title: Re: breaking a love charm cast by someone else towards me?
replies.content:
Sure. Get a small doll of the same sex of the other person, and baptize it in the other person's name. Then pack it in a box and mail it, with no return address or a false one, to the other person, c/o General Delivery, Hell, Michigan 48169.

(I learned that working from a couple of Eastern Star ladies in a small town in the Appalachians; they said it's a foolproof way to get someone to leave you alone. Give it a try.)

replies.title: Re: breaking a love charm cast by someone else towards me?
replies.content: Thank you, JMG! This has the added benefit of amusing me to no end.

replies.title: Re: breaking a love charm cast by someone else towards me?
replies.content: That's part of the point. Laughter is a powerful tool for banishing. If you're under magical attack, read or watch something that makes you laugh until your sides hurt, and the mage who is attacking you is going to have a miserable time of it.

replies.title: Re: breaking a love charm cast by someone else towards me?
replies.content:
Hell is in Michigan? I had not guessed! I would have assumed Mississippi somewhere, with not much there besides a crossroads and some big black dogs.

Sincerely,
Jessi Thompson
anotheramethyst

replies.title: Re: breaking a love charm cast by someone else towards me?
replies.content: Hell is the real name of a real town in Michigan, Jessi.  IIRC, it's a bit north and west of Ann Arbor.

replies.title: Re: breaking a love charm cast by someone else towards me?
replies.content:
Dear Anonymous,
I feel your pain. Obsessive thinking, a drama triangle, ill effects that last and last, someone acting like an idiot -- I've been there too, not so long ago, and like you I also thought that maybe I was the victim of a love spell. In fact, I started to read about spells and magic for that reason -- to learn how to break the spell. I didn't try JMG's doll method, but I tried a lot of other things, which would work for a while, and then not work. I think I'm at the end of the troubles now, maybe, so I'll tell you some things that helped me.

I don't know how long love spells work, I'm not a magician. But scientists have shown that romantic love -- the intense, obsessive kind -- typically has a life span of no more than two years. After that, it fades away. When I was suffering, I would think about this fact, and it would comfort me. I would think, "Only 8 more months to go" and so on.

I also tried prayer: in place of obsessive thinking, I would try to quiet my mind in meditation, then I would pray that God gives that person whatever it is they need most to be happy. I would think of the nicest things, the best of blessings, and ask God to give it to that person in whatever way was most appropriate for them to receive the blessings. By praying sincerely for the person who had caused me so much trouble and pain, I was able to overcome my anger towards them and feel love for them again.

Finally, astrology -- Venus retrograde from Oct 6 thru Nov 16 of this year - tendency to review past loves - with Venus now in Scorpio, the obsession may continue for a while - in 7 days, the planet of love leaves the retrograde zone, and this is a time when hopefully we can transcend the past.

Peace to you, dear anonymous, I will pray for your healing & freedom.

replies.title: Re: breaking a love charm cast by someone else towards me?
replies.content:
Thank you for these heartfelt reflections and suggestions, Anonymous.

The astrological correlations to all this have been potent and this is an ancient connection (many odd past life memories spontaneously emerged - a lot of heart break and grief had been between us). I do my best to wish him well, and to dig deeply and mean it from the depths of my being. I don't like being so irritated with it all, but the months of pestering do add up, as you can appreciate. Yet I have noticed that wishing all beings well seems to form a kind of protection in and of itself.

thank you again and may you be at ease in all ways,
Anon :-)

comments.title: Healing amulet /charm /prayer for a family curse?
comments.content:
We're having a terrible time with our five year old. Mostly, it's normal boundary establishment-new school stuff, but there is something else that I think of as the Family Curse. The men on my mom's side have been angry men for as long as memory allows; very intelligent, usually highly humanitarian, sensitive and justice oriented, but therefore capable of great rage at injustice (usually accurately perceived, mores the shame- the world is a very unfair place), and since men are culturally only allowed to feel anger... They feel so much, and it all comes out rage. Domestic abuse, child abuse, foster care,jail, the works. Screaming cases of manic depression. The women, we have the same anger, but being allowed to feel, end up functional and socially acceptable, just transmute it into being anxious and neurotic instead. I am The One Who Showed It Could Be Done, though, the one who conquered the anxiety disorder, who finished school, who got the job and the healthy marriage... DNA isn't destiny, bla bla..

When I was pregnant, I knew it would be a boy, and hoped he'd take after his dad. Nope. He's all mine. Oh the anger. Once I asked my tarot whether I should do some sort of banishing of negative energy in our house? A smudge? I got the clearest "oh frack no, don't even think it, that's the worst thing you could do" that I've ever gotten from such an interpretive oracle! After last week's commenter sent their brother in law away after simply trying to drive out bad energy, I see why that could have been bad.

But I feel like somehow this was supposed to stop with me, maybe I shouldn't have had kids at all, but I told myself I was being superstitious. I can't shake the feeling that somehow it's something I haven't done, haven't fixed, but could have. I'm all over trying to use the best psychological science on parenting I can, working on my own stuff... The school counselor is helping him with emotional regulation... But I feel like it's bigger than me, there's this feeling of I don't know how to say it other than karma or curse,or like a stray demon that follows my family around, and I could close the door, if I could just figure out where it is. How would I find out what magical threads are even safe to pull here? Is the safest thing just prayer?
The poor kid.

replies.title: Re: Healing amulet /charm /prayer for a family curse?
replies.content: This isn't magic-related per se - or maybe, if you read between the lines, it is - but I think you might find a thought-provoking perspective on anger, particularly the kind of rage-at-injustice you describe, here: https://www.theatlantic.com/magazine/archive/2019/01/charles-duhigg-american-anger/576424/

replies.title: Re: Healing amulet /charm /prayer for a family curse?
replies.content: Good stuff. Also Mark Ames' Going Postal. Some quotes: https://en.wikiquote.org/wiki/Mark_Ames (contains some "bad words").

replies.title: Re: Healing amulet /charm /prayer for a family curse?
replies.content: Huh. Thanks for that... That definitely gives me something to think about when I compare it to answers I formerly thought were confusing from previous divination and meditations. Huh!

replies.title: Re: Healing amulet /charm /prayer for a family curse?
replies.content: Prayer is your best approach here, as you don't know what's going on. Ask the deity of your choice for help, and listen for any intuitive prompts you may receive.

replies.title: Re: Healing amulet /charm /prayer for a family curse?
replies.content:
Normally I wouldn't presume to butt into something like this but if you're at all open to homeopathic care, there is a highly-regarded group specializing in treatment of autistic and severely disturbed children. Family members often also seek care once the child is improving. Properly done, it can remove generational taints.

They provide reasonably priced telephone consults if needed. You can review their work online: Robert Ullman and Judyth Reichenberg-Ullman.

replies.title: Re: Healing amulet /charm /prayer for a family curse?
replies.content:
Have you heard of Denny Johnson the founder of Rayid? He published "The Nature of Birth Order" describing the distinctive qualities of children, influence of parents and grandparents and how to enhance their potential.
Check it out:
https://rayid.com/denny-johnson/

All the best

replies.title: Re: Healing amulet /charm /prayer for a family curse?
replies.content: I think my mom has his book, thanks for the reminder!

comments.title: Will
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Do you have any tips on training the will?
And do you know any good books on this subject?

About ten years ago I read 'The Act of Will' by Roberto Assagioli which was quite a fascinating book. Not that I agree with everything he writes, but it was an eye-opener at the time. Recently the will grasped my attention again and now I'm looking for some more information. Your thoughts on the subject will be greatly appreciated.

replies.title: Re: Will
replies.content:
The word 'will' can mean several things. In one sense it is used to mean something like 'discipline' - stoicism, perseverance. In another sense it is used to mean something like 'agency' - choosing a goal and acting on it. These meanings are not identical, but neither are they unrelated.

However I've noticed a paradox that occurs when people treat them as if they were opposed. Authority figures often set up a false dilemma between discipline and agency by defining 'discipline' as docility - uncomplainingly doing as you're told, stoically tolerating your lack of autonomy. Too many bad experiences with manipulation of that kind can turn someone off 'discipline' entirely. However there lies the paradox: if you can no longer bring yourself to voluntarily persevere, that itself is a major diminishment of your autonomy, and it will limit how successfully you can exercise agency towards your goals.

I think for some people, 'training the will' is less about learning how to endure - they've already done a lot of that for everyone's purposes but their own! - and more about learning how to exercise agency: to identify your own goals and refine them, to put thought into action, and to resist coercion.

replies.title: Re: Will
replies.content:
There used to be a huge amount of literature out there offering exercises for developing the will. I've read a good bit of it, and you don't need the books, because they all use the same basic principle: you train the will by choosing to do things for no reason.

I mean that literally. Right now, get up, cross the room, and touch the far wall, then come back to the computer and go on. Pointless? Sure; so is lifting a barbell. The principle is the same. Choose a deliberately pointless thing to do, and do it. Then do another one. Then do the same thing every morning for a week. You can come up with any number of exercises on your own.

One I used was to choose, on a map, a place in the city I lived where I'd never been; decide to be there at some casually chosen time; make the appointment with myself; work out all the details of getting there -- since I don't drive, that usually involved a fair mastery of public transit and a willingness to walk; then be there exactly on time. It won't take long before you have a well-developed will that you can then use for practical purposes as well as magical ones.

replies.title: Re: Will
replies.content:
Thanks for the exercise.

- S. T. Silva.

replies.title: Re: Will
replies.content: How about an inconsistent will? I'm pretty iron-willed in some areas (finances, health) but not so much in other areas (time management, work).

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

My question this week is a variation on the "should I practice magic with young children in the house?"

I am curious on the effects of magical practice on young animals, particularly kittens.

For the first time in nearly 19 years, we are a catless household. We said goodbye to our two elderly feline gentlemen over the last couple of years. We're currently taking a break from having pets, but will likely welcome another kitten or two in in the spring or summer.

I'm not terribly worried about it, as I practice in a room with a door I can close to keep them out. We'll find out eventually. I'm more curious about the general effect.

Thanks!
Bonnie

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Cats thrive on magic. Back when we had a cat, whenever I would set up a portable altar for a working, she'd come trotting over, curl up underneath the altar, and go to sleep. Your kitten or kittens should be fine; they're not vulnerable the way that young humans are.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's good to hear. I suspected it would probably be ok. Our former cats seemed fine with it and always behaved well around the practice and its accoutrements, though they were young adults by the time I started. Neither showed any signs of discomfort or other negative reactions.

Curling up under the altar to nap - I love it.

comments.title: Visualization - how to keep it safe
comments.content:
This question is primarily about safety, but any other insight would also be very welcome.

I have been working on visualizing while meditating. I'm Catholic, so the logical place to start seemed to be with the rosary: visualizing the mysteries is a very well-established form of interacting with the rosary. However, there are not a standard set of images, and many of the ones I found were too detailed, or just not right in one way or another, and I found that I did not have a good reference set of visualizations. I decided to create a simple set for myself: just a plain pattern, with colors varying by mystery. (Deliberately vague: I'll detail the patterns and colors if needed.) I used the set daily for a month,and it seemed to work well. I found it easier to visualize the images, I had to work less at keeping them from morphing, and I was calmer and more able to deal with what life threw at me.

Then I began to worry; if the patterns in the notory art are critical as patterns, and the mandala patterns of Tibetan Buddhism are the same, is there a risk of someone who's as ignorant as I developing patterns and meditating on them? Is there anything to watch out for? The rosary is naturally protective, but I still want to be reasonably cautious.

replies.title: Re: Visualization - how to keep it safe
replies.content: Shouldn't you be directing this question toward your spiritual director, or at least an experienced Catholic who knows their way around your tradition? I'm not an expert in everything, and since you've got the advantage of a well-established religious tradition with a couple of thousand years of experience under its belt, you should take such a question to a priest, religious, or experienced layperson, not to a Druid!

replies.title: Re: Visualization - how to keep it safe
replies.content:
Thank you!

I have asked, and no one has thought it a problem (just odd, because they either don't visualize the mysteries or can visualize fully-detailed images). However, none of them are to my knowledge even somewhat knowledgeable about pattern-visualization magic; that's not the way any Catholic ritual works to my knowledge. I thought it might be worth asking someone aware of the notory art for a second opinion. Your response makes me much more comfortable with continuing to use my own patterns for now and continuing to work toward the full imagery.

SamChevre

replies.title: Re: Visualization - how to keep it safe
replies.content: The notory art is extremely specialized. Lots of people have been doing lots of visualization for a very long time with no ill effects, so I suspect you'll be fine.

comments.title: Visualization - how to keep it safe
comments.content: My apologies for not signing the above question on visualizing rosary mysteries: that is me, SamChevre.

comments.title: Herbal Resources
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

I'm going to pick up a copy of your Encyclopedia of Natural Magic, but I also wanted to see if you knew off the top of your head some texts that deal specifically with the humors. I was looking at Cunningham's encyclopedia, but I see that he doesn't mention anything about herbs being dry, moist, etc. I know you have a section on that in your CGD, but I'd love to check out some other good references.

Do you happen to know a good version of Culpeper's Herbal? Looking around, it seems there are some pretty strong opinions about certain reproductions being quite poor, but I know he deals with those kinds of classifications.

I'm interested in herbs generally and have made a few tinctures, but I'm also planning on making some infused jojoba oil with particular herbs and resins associated with the different elements to finish my wand, with the idea that the elemental qualities of the herbs will sink into the wood, hopefully amplifying it's own qualities. Does that seem like a sound idea?

Thank you,
David

replies.title: Re: Herbal Resources
replies.content:
Culpeper mostly deals with the planetary attributions of herbs. I got most of the humoral information I have out of medieval texts in English translation or Latin critical editions. I don't know of a good modern source.

Your plan with the wand sounds like a good idea. I've used infused oils for a range of magical purposes with good results.

replies.title: Re: Herbal Resources
replies.content:
The below isn't what you asked for, and may be old news to you, but I'm offering it case it may be of some use. It is about contemporary Western medical herbalism.

Traditional Western herbalism classifies herbs in multiple binaries. My herbalism teacher told me that for clinical purposes (not magical, because that's not what the course was about), the most important qualities are hot/cold and moist/dry. These are important in treatment of physical problems because, for example, if someone has a cold constitution and you had a choice of several herbs that could be used to treat their complaint, you would prefer a warming herb so as not to put them out of balance. If the complaint was a dry cough, you would give them moist herbs; if it was a productive cough you might give them a drying herb.

Here is an introductory book by a living herbalist of this sort: The Practice of Traditional Western Herbalism, by Matthew Wood. The subtitle is Basic Doctrine, Energetics, and Classification. 2004, North Atlantic Books, Berkeley CA. ISBN # 978-1-55643-503-4. Trade paperback cover price is $18.95 US.

The bibliography lists Salmon, William, M.D. Botanologia, or the English Herbal, London, I. Hawkes, 1710 in the same section (about qualities) as a book about Culpeper by Graeme Tobyn. The edition of Culpeper he lists in the bibliography is a 1994 reprint of the 1814 London edition.

Humors have only five entries in the index of Wood's book.

comments.title: Two questions
comments.content:
Archdruid,

In the Druidry Handbook and Druid Magic Handbook you lay out a simple method of divination that lays the fews out past, present, and future to mimic the pattern for the three rays of light. Are there other patterns we can use?

Second question. Cooking can be very magical if done properly, I've noticed that the system of elements laid out in the two druidry books provides a basic symbolic pattern for both healing (creating balance in the body by insuring that the seven elements are in balance) and cooking (creating a delicious meal by insuring the seven elements are in harmony). If I were to cook meals to help heal or make me healthier, and someone else were to eat that meal, would that be considered casting a spell upon someone who wasn't a willing participant?

Regards,

Syfen

replies.title: Re: Two questions
replies.content:
1) Of course. Invent some!

2) It would be polite to let your guest know that you planned the meal to benefit the health of anyone who shares in it. I suspect they'll be delighted, but you never know!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
As science fiction story-inspiration the Mandela Effect is pretty useful. Is it the sort of thing magic could plausibly cause?

BoysMom

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Well, in a sense. It could certainly convince people that they'd heard something in the past that they hadn't actually heard, or convince them to misremember something that they did hear a good many years ago.

That's what's going on with the so-called Mandela effect's original example. Steve Biko, who was considerably more famous than Nelson Mandela during his life, died in a South African prison in 1977; he was then largely forgotten outside of South Africa, but dim memories of his career and death got mapped onto Nelson Mandela once Mandela, rather than Biko, was the name people in US liberal circles associated with the struggle to end apartheid. Since the mind manufactures its memories out of spotty cues every time you remember something, that sort of mismatch happens all the time -- and yes, magic could be used to make it more likely.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I have a personal memory mismatch with Isaiah 11:6, which annoys me no end, because when I learned it as a child, I'm sure it was ". . .and the lion will lie down with the lamb, and a little child will lead them." and that stuck in my head because of course the Lion of Judah and the Lamb of God are both names for Christ, and Isaiah's pun was cool, and I'm quite sure there was a crossstich illustrating the verse on the wall of the first grade Sunday School classroom. (I wasn't less odd as a child than today, you'll note!)

Thank you much, Brother Greer, I'll have to see if I can work that out as a plot device. Or if anyone else wants to, do feel free to have at it.

BoysMom

replies.title: Lions and lambs
replies.content:
That’s wild. I have the same memory. I just now googled that verse, to see what on earth you are talking about. Where did all this stuff about wolves and leopards come from???

Or I guess maybe I should be asking where did the lion and lamb stuff come from?
